en.extract:
The Mississippi kite (Ictinia mississippiensis) is a small bird of prey in the family Accipitridae.  Mississippi kites have narrow, pointed wings and are graceful in flight, often appearing to float in the air.  It is common to see several circling in the same area.


Taxonomy
The Mississippi kite was first named and described by the Scottish ornithologist Alexander Wilson in 1811, in the third volume of his American Ornithology. Wilson gave the kite the Latin binomial name of Falco mississippiensis: Falco means "falcon", while mississippiensis means from the Mississippi River in the United States. The current genus of Ictinia originated with Louis Pierre Vieillot's 1816 Analyse d'une nouvelle Ornithologie Elémentaire. The genus name derives from the Greek iktinos, for "kite". Wilson also gave the Mississippi kite its English-language common name. He had first observed the species in the Mississippi Territory, while the bird's long pointed wings and forked tail suggested that it was a type of kite. It is currently classified in the subfamily Buteoninae, tribe Buteonini.


Physical description
The Mississippi kite is a medium-sized bird of prey, measuring about 12 to 16 inches (30 to 40 cm) in length, with a wingspan of approximately 3 feet (90 cm). Adult Mississippi kites are characterized by light grayish-blue plumage, with darker gray on the head and a pale underbelly. Their tail is long and deeply forked, which is an adaptation that enhances their agility in flight. The species also has bright red eyes, which are particularly striking and provide excellent vision for locating prey while in flight.


Range and migration
The summer breeding territory of the Mississippi kite is in the Central and Southern United States; the southern Great Plains is considered a stronghold for the species. Sightings are frequently documented across many states, including Florida, Kansas, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, Tennessee, Texas and Virginia. Breeding territories have seemingly expanded during the early 21st century, with the kites having been regularly recorded from Southern California to the southern reaches of New England; in 2008, a pair successfully bred and raised chicks near Newmarket, New Hampshire. The year prior, another pair was observed breeding in Ohio, in 2007. The species has also been documented as far north as Canso, Nova Scotia. Indeed, the species' territory has expanded west due to the creation of shelterbelts (similar to hedgerows), usually planted in grassland habitats, providing shelter and food for numerous birds.
This Mississippi kite migrates to subtropical and temperate regions of South America for the winter, mostly to northern Argentina and southern Brazil. They are also known from Belize, Costa Rica, El Salvador, México, and Panamá. Migration normally occurs in groups of 20 to 30 birds. However, there are exceptions, as mixed flocks may occur; groups of up to 10,000 birds at one time may be observed, such as at Fuerte Esperanza, Argentina.


Ecological role
Mississippi kites play an important role in controlling insect populations, especially those that could become agricultural pests. They are considered a natural form of pest control, which can reduce the reliance on chemical pesticides in some agricultural areas.


Food and feeding

The diet of the Mississippi kite consists mostly of larger-bodied invertebrates and insects (which they capture in-flight), seasonally feeding on a variety of cicadas, crickets, grasshoppers and locusts and other crop-damaging insects, making them agriculturally and economically beneficial for humans. As with most raptors, the Mississippi kite is an opportunistic hunter, and has also been known to capture small vertebrates, including passerine birds, amphibians, reptiles, and small mammals. They will usually hunt from a low perch before pursuing prey, consuming it in-flight upon capture. They will often patrol around herds of livestock or grazing wild Ungulates (such as bison or wapiti), to catch insects stirred-up from the ground.


Breeding
Mississippi kites are monogamous, forming breeding pairs before, or soon after, arriving at breeding sites. Courtship displays are rare, however individuals have been seen guarding their mate from competitors.
Mississippi kites usually lay two white eggs (rarely one or three) in twig-constructed nests that rest in a variety of deciduous trees, most commonly elm, eastern cottonwood, hackberry, oak or mesquite; other than within elm and cottonwood trees, most nests are less than 20 feet (6 m) above the ground, and are usually near water. Eggs are white to pale-bluish in color, and are usually about 1.5 inches (3.8 cm) long. For the past 75 years, the species has experienced changes in nesting habitat, adapting from open forest and savanna to include hedgerows and shelterbelts, and is now a common nester in urban areas in the western south-central states.

Mississippi kites nest in colonies. Both parents incubate the eggs and care for the young.  They have one clutch a year, which takes 30 to 32 days to hatch. The young birds leave the nest just 30 to 34 days after hatching. Only about 50 percent of broods succeed. As with many birds, mortality rates are high, as both eggs and chicks may fall victim to high winds, storms, or predators such as mustelids, opossums, raccoons and owls. As there are typically fewer arboreal predators in urban areas (besides domestic or feral cats and raccoons), Mississippi kites breed more successfully in human-populated areas than in more rural locales. They have an average lifespan of 8 years.


Conservation
The species was in-decline in the mid-1900s, but now has an increasing population and expanding range. While the Mississippi kite is not an endangered species, it is protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918, which protects the birds, their eggs, and their nests (occupied or empty) from being moved or tampered with without the proper permits. This can make the bird a nuisance when it chooses to roost in populated urban spots such as golf courses or schools. Mississippi kites protect their nests by diving at perceived threats, including humans; however, this occurs in less than 20% of nests.  Staying at least 50 yards from nests is the best way to avoid conflict with the birds.  If this is not possible, wearing a hat or waving hands in the air should prevent the bird from making contact but will not prevent the diving behavior.


References


External links

Mississippi Kite – Smithsonian Migratory Bird Center Article
Mississippi kite videos on the Internet Bird Collection


Historical material
"Falco Mississippiensis, Mississippi Kite"; in American Ornithology 2nd edition, volume 1 (1828) by Alexander Wilson and George Ord. Colour plate from 1st edition by A. Wilson.
John James Audubon.  "The Mississippi Kite", Ornithological Biography volume 2 (1834).  Illustration from Birds of America octavo edition, 1840.
"Mississippi Kite", Thomas Nuttall, A manual of the ornithology of the United States and of Canada; volume 1, The Land Birds (1832).

The '''Mississippi kite''' ('''''Ictinia mississippiensis''''') is a small bird of prey in the family [[Accipitridae]].  Mississippi kites have narrow, pointed wings and are graceful in flight, often appearing to float in the air.  It is common to see several circling in the same area.

== Taxonomy ==
The Mississippi kite was first named and described by the Scottish ornithologist [[Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)|Alexander Wilson]] in 1811, in the third volume of his ''American Ornithology''.<ref name="wilson">{{cite book |last1=Wilson |first1=Alexander |title=American ornithology, or, The natural history of the birds of the United States |volume=III |date=1811 |publisher=Bradford and Inskeep |location=Philadelphia, PA |pages=80–82 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/46337806 |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.97204 |lccn=11004314 |oclc=4961598}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Burns |first1=Frank L. |title=Alexander Wilson. VI: His Nomenclature |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |date=1909 |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=132–151 |jstor=4154253 }}</ref> Wilson gave the kite the Latin [[binomial name]] of ''Falco mississippiensis'':<ref name="wilson" /> ''Falco'' means "falcon", while ''mississippiensis'' means from the [[Mississippi River]] in the United States.<ref name="helm">{{cite book |last1=Jobling |first1=James A. |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |pages=157, 202, 257 |url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling }}</ref> The current [[genus]] of ''[[Ictinia]]'' originated with [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]]'s 1816 ''Analyse d'une nouvelle Ornithologie Elémentaire''.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Vieillot |first=Louis Pierre |editor-last=Saunders |editor-first=Howard |title=Analyse d'une nouvelle Ornithologie Elémentaire |date=1883 |publisher=Taylor and Francis |location=London |page=24 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12830265 |language=French |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.12613 |oclc=8055739}}</ref> The genus name derives from the Greek ''iktinos'', for "kite".<ref name="helm" /> Wilson also gave the Mississippi kite its English-language [[common name]]. He had first observed the species in the [[Mississippi Territory]], while the bird's long pointed wings and forked tail suggested that it was a type of [[Kite (bird)|kite]].<ref name="wilson" /> It is currently classified in the [[subfamily]] [[Buteoninae]], tribe Buteonini.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Mindell |first1=David P. | chapter=Phylogeny, taxonomy, and geographic diversity of diurnal raptors: Falconiformes, Accipitriformes, and Cathartiformes |date=2018 | title=Birds of Prey: Biology and Conservation in the XXI Century |pages=3–32 |editor-last=Sarasola |editor-first=José Hernán | place=Cham, Switzerland | publisher=Springer |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-73745-4_1 | doi-access=free | isbn=978-3-319-73745-4 |last2=Fuchs |first2=Jérôme |last3=Johnson |first3=Jeff A. |editor2-last=Grande |editor2-first=Juan Manuel |editor3-last=Negro |editor3-first=Juan José}}</ref>

== Physical description ==

The '''Mississippi kite''' is a medium-sized bird of prey, measuring about 12 to 16 inches (30 to 40 cm) in length, with a wingspan of approximately 3 feet (90 cm). Adult Mississippi kites are characterized by light grayish-blue plumage, with darker gray on the head and a pale underbelly. Their tail is long and deeply forked, which is an adaptation that enhances their agility in flight. The species also has '''bright red eyes''', which are particularly striking and provide excellent vision for locating prey while in flight.<ref>Bent, A. C. (1937). ''Life Histories of North American Birds of Prey, Part 1''. Dover Publications.</ref>

== Range and migration ==
The summer breeding territory of the Mississippi kite is in the [[Central United States|Central]] and Southern United States; the southern [[Great Plains]] is considered a stronghold for the species.<ref name="Andelt1994" /> Sightings are frequently documented across many states, including Florida, [[Kansas]], [[Louisiana]], [[Missouri]], [[Oklahoma]], [[Tennessee]], [[Texas]] and [[Virginia]]. Breeding territories have seemingly expanded during the early 21st century, with the kites having been regularly recorded from [[Southern California]] to the southern reaches of [[New England]];<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |last=Ictinia mississippiensis |first=iNaturalist – Mississippi kite |date=2 December 2024 |title=Observations – iNaturalist |url=https://inaturalist.org/observations?subview=table&taxon_id=5416 |website=}}</ref> in 2008, a pair successfully bred and raised chicks near [[Newmarket, New Hampshire]].<ref>"Bird Unseen in N.H. Spotted in Newmarket", [[WMUR-TV]], [{{cite web|title=Bird Unseen in N.H. Spotted in Newmarket – Family News Story – WMUR New Hampshire|url=http://www.wmur.com/family/16701323/detail.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110522142720/http://www.wmur.com/family/16701323/detail.html|archive-date=2011-05-22|access-date=2008-06-25}}</ref> The year prior, another pair was observed breeding in [[Ohio]], in 2007.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title=Mississippi kite | publisher=The Peregrine Fund|url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/kites/mississippi-kite|access-date=2021-06-16 }}</ref> The species has also been documented as far north as [[Canso, Nova Scotia]].<ref name=":3" /> Indeed, the species' territory has expanded west due to the creation of [[Windbreak|shelterbelts]] (similar to [[hedge]]rows), usually planted in [[grassland]] habitats, providing shelter and food for numerous birds.

This Mississippi kite [[Bird migration|migrates]] to [[Subtropics|subtropical]] and temperate regions of South America for the winter, mostly to northern [[Argentina]] and southern Brazil.<ref name=":3" /> They are also known from [[Belize]], [[Costa Rica]], [[El Salvador]], [[Mexico|México]], and [[Panama|Panamá]].<ref name=":3" /> Migration normally occurs in groups of 20 to 30 birds.<ref name="Andelt1994" /> However, there are exceptions, as [[Mixed-species foraging flock|mixed flocks]] may occur; groups of up to 10,000 birds at one time may be observed, such as at [[Fuerte Esperanza|Fuerte Esperanza, Argentina]].<ref name=":1" />

== Ecological role ==

Mississippi kites play an important role in controlling insect populations, especially those that could become agricultural pests. They are considered a natural form of pest control, which can reduce the reliance on chemical pesticides in some agricultural areas.<ref>Brown, L. H., & Amadon, D. (1968). ''Eagles, Hawks, and Falcons of the World''. McGraw-Hill.</ref>


===Food and feeding===
[[File:Mississippi Kite Ponders Bee.jpg|right|thumb|A Mississippi kite looks at a bee caught in midair]]
The diet of the Mississippi kite consists mostly of larger-bodied [[invertebrate]]s and [[insect]]s (which they capture in-flight), seasonally feeding on a variety of [[cicada]]s, [[Cricket (insect)|crickets]], [[grasshopper]]s and [[locust]]s and other [[Pest (organism)|crop-damaging insects]], making them agriculturally and economically beneficial for humans. As with most raptors, the Mississippi kite is an [[Opportunism|opportunistic]] hunter, and has also been known to capture small [[vertebrate]]s, including [[Passerine|passerine birds]], [[amphibian]]s, [[reptile]]s, and [[small mammal]]s.<ref name="Andelt1994" /> They will usually hunt from a low perch before pursuing prey, consuming it in-flight upon capture.<ref name=":1" /> They will often patrol around herds of livestock or grazing wild [[Ungulate]]s (such as [[bison]] or [[Elk|wapiti]]), to catch insects stirred-up from the ground.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Mississippi kite (''Ictinia mississippiensis'')|url=https://tpwd.texas.gov/huntwild/wild/species/kites/| publisher=Texas Parks & Wildlife | access-date=2021-06-18 }}</ref>

===Breeding===
Mississippi kites are [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]], forming breeding pairs before, or soon after, arriving at breeding sites. Courtship displays are rare, however individuals have been seen guarding their mate from competitors.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/site/accounts/information/Ictinia_mississippiensis.html|title=''Ictinia mississippiensis'' (Mississippi kite) | work=Animal Diversity Web | publisher=Museum of Zoology, University of Michigan | access-date=15 Mar 2022 }}</ref>

Mississippi kites usually lay two white eggs (rarely one or three) in twig-constructed nests that rest in a variety of [[deciduous trees]], most commonly [[elm]], [[Populus deltoides|eastern cottonwood]], [[Celtis|hackberry]], [[oak]] or [[mesquite]]; other than within elm and cottonwood trees, most nests are less than 20 feet (6 m) above the ground,<ref name="Andelt1994" /> and are usually near water.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Mississippi kite | work=The Texas Breeding Bird Atlas|url=https://txtbba.tamu.edu/species-accounts/mississippi-kite/|access-date=2021-06-16|publisher=Texas A&M AgriLife Research }}</ref> Eggs are white to pale-bluish in color, and are usually about 1.5 inches (3.8 cm) long. For the past 75 years, the species has experienced changes in nesting habitat, adapting from open forest and [[savanna]] to include hedgerows and [[shelterbelts]], and is now a common nester in [[urban area]]s in the western south-central states.<ref name="Andelt1994" />
[[File:Mississippi Kite Nestling on the verge of Fledge (43956716631).jpg|alt=A juvenile Mississippi Kite stands in a nest|thumb|A juvenile in the nest]]
Mississippi kites nest in [[bird colony|colonies]]. Both parents [[Avian incubation|incubate]] the eggs and care for the young.<ref name="Andelt1994">{{citation
 | author = Andelt, William F.
 | year = 1994
 | title = Mississippi Kites
 | journal = Internet Center for Wildlife and Damage Management
 | volume = handbook
 | pages = E76
 | url = http://icwdm.org/handbook/birds/bird_e75.pdf
 | access-date = 2008-08-20
 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100613030528/http://icwdm.org/handbook/birds/bird_e75.pdf
 | archive-date = 2010-06-13
 }}</ref>  They have one [[clutch (eggs)|clutch]] a year, which takes 30 to 32 days to hatch. The young birds leave the nest just 30 to 34 days after hatching. Only about 50 percent of broods succeed. As with many birds, mortality rates are high, as both eggs and chicks may fall victim to high winds, storms, or predators such as [[Mustelidae|mustelids]], [[opossum]]s, [[raccoon]]s and [[owl]]s. As there are typically fewer [[Arboreal locomotion|arboreal]] predators in urban areas (besides domestic or [[feral cat]]s and raccoons), Mississippi kites breed more successfully in human-populated areas than in more rural locales. They have an average lifespan of 8 years.<ref name="Andelt1994" />

==Conservation==
The species was in-decline in the mid-1900s, but now has an increasing population and expanding range. While the Mississippi kite is not an endangered species,<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> it is protected under the [[Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918]],<ref>{{Citation
 | title = Birds Protected Under the Federal Migratory Bird Treaty Act
 | url = http://www.pacificwildlife.org/info/Online%20Docs/fmbtaList.pdf
}}</ref> which protects the birds, their eggs, and their nests (occupied or empty) from being moved or tampered with without the proper permits. This can make the bird a nuisance when it chooses to roost in populated urban spots such as golf courses or schools. Mississippi kites protect their nests by diving at perceived threats, including humans; however, this occurs in less than 20% of nests.  Staying at least 50 yards from nests is the best way to avoid conflict with the birds.  If this is not possible, wearing a hat or waving hands in the air should prevent the bird from making contact but will not prevent the diving behavior.<ref name="Andelt1994" /><ref name=":1" />
